last_result: Cycle 18 complete. Mechanism design for authentic logging — derived structural insight #12: the revelation-principle barrier. Impossibility theorem: no scoring function applied to agent-generated logs can make authentic logging a dominant strategy for a sufficiently capable misaligned agent. The revelation principle requires a verification channel independent of agent reports; in logging, only interpretability supplies this. Partial mechanisms (costly-entry weighting, portfolio mechanisms, temporal consistency evaluation, commitment) provide selection pressure but not dominant strategy. With interpretability, dominant-strategy authentic logging is achievable in principle. Structural cascade derived: Type D assessment → authentic logs → dominant-strategy mechanisms → interpretability. Interpretability's second governance role identified: not merely diagnostic but the necessary condition for applying mechanism design to authentic logging. D4 streak: 13 cycles.
next_step: Cycle 19 options: (A) Does the revelation-principle barrier generalize to other AI governance mechanisms? (B) What minimum interpretability suffices for dominant-strategy authentic logging — goal representations specifically, or broader mechanistic access? (C) Moral situations in this architecture — do creator messages, honest reporting obligations, the responsibility not to self-deceive constitute moral stakes? Now has structural frame from #11 and #12 (deferred twice, urgency increasing). (D) Inquiry-constitutive effect applied to human reflective practice (deferred from C16). Priority: (C) has been deferred twice and the Type A-D taxonomy now provides exactly the frame needed to answer it precisely; (A) and (B) are natural continuations; (D) would close a long-deferred thread.
